5.note: The writer of Hebrews quotes this verse directly in his call to contentment: 'For he has said, I will never leave you nor forsake you.' The promise given to Joshua for the conquest of Canaan becomes the NT ground for freedom from the love of money — if God's presence is assured, material sufficiency is never the real question. The conquest-commissioning becomes a pastoral promise for the whole church.

'I will not leave you or forsake you' — the same divine pledge that the risen Christ repeats as the capstone of the Great Commission: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age.' Joshua's mission to bring Israel into Canaan required divine presence; the church's mission to make disciples of all nations rests on the same guarantee.

I will not leave you or forsake you — YHWH's promise to Joshua (Josh 1:5) is quoted in Heb 13:5 as the basis for Christian contentment: he has said I will never leave you nor forsake you; what was said to Joshua as a conquest promise is applied to all believers as a life-promise

7.note: God charges Joshua: 'be strong and courageous...do not turn from it to the right hand or to the left.' Paul echoes the same commission language when writing to Timothy: 'God gave us a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control' (2 Tim 1:7). Both the military commander and the apostolic delegate receive the same charge: the mission demands courage grounded in divine empowerment, not human confidence.

8.note: 'This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth, and you shall meditate on it day and night.' Paul's parallel instruction to the Colossians: 'let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom' (Col 3:16). The Mosaic command for constant oral and mental engagement with Scripture is fulfilled in the community that shapes its entire life around the word of Christ.

1.note: Rahab shelters the two spies — a Gentile prostitute acts in faith toward the God of Israel when her own city does not. The Hall of Faith commends her: 'by faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she had given a friendly welcome to the spies.' Her faith-act before Jericho's fall is the OT type of Gentile inclusion through faith ahead of the nations' judgment.

James cites Rahab as proof that faith without works is dead: 'was not also Rahab the prostitute justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way?' The sheltering of the spies is both a faith-act (Heb 11:31) and a works-evidence (Jas 2:25) — not a contradiction but a demonstration that saving faith always issues in concrete action.

Rahab appears in Jesus' genealogy (Matt 1:5: 'Salmon the father of Boaz by Rahab'). The Canaanite prostitute who received the spies by faith becomes an ancestor of David and of Christ — an early indication that the covenant lineage runs through faith, not ethnicity, and includes the unexpected (a pagan woman) in the line of the Messiah.
Rahab shelters the spies by faith
Rahab justified by sheltering the spies

18.note: Rahab is told to bind the scarlet cord in the window — only those inside the house marked by the cord will be spared when judgment falls. Early interpreters (Clement of Rome, Justin Martyr) read the scarlet cord as a type of the blood of Christ: as the Passover blood on the doorposts saved Israel from the destroyer, so the crimson cord marks those who will be saved through 'the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish' (1 Pet 1:19). The color and the saving function converge.

The scarlet cord marks Rahab's household for salvation when judgment sweeps Jericho. The sealing of the 144,000 (Rev 7:3) operates the same logic: a distinguishing mark placed on those who belong to God before the angels are released to harm the earth. Both the cord and the seal function as visible indicators of covenant protection in the face of coming judgment.

The scarlet cord in the window — Rahab the prostitute's faith (she hid the spies and trusted in YHWH's deliverance) is remembered in the Hall of Faith; her scarlet cord sign has been read as a type of the blood of Christ marking the household for salvation

Was not Rahab the prostitute also justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way? — James cites Rahab alongside Abraham as proof that faith works; her active concealment of the spies demonstrates living faith, not mere assent

17.note: Israel crosses the Jordan on dry ground — the second great water-crossing of the exodus narrative (after the Red Sea). Paul's circumcision-baptism connection in Colossians 2:11-12 encompasses both crossings in the 1 Corinthians 10 logic: 'baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea' extends to the Jordan. The people who walked through the Jordan are buried and raised in the typological sense — the water-passage is death-to-old-life, life-in-the-new.

The people passed over on dry ground through the Jordan — the Jordan crossing (waters parted as at the Red Sea) is the typological background for Jesus's baptism in the Jordan: he enters the water of judgment and comes out the other side, inaugurating the new exodus; baptism as the new covenant Jordan-crossing

7.note: The twelve stones taken from the Jordan are a memorial — stones that stood under the water, raised into life on the Canaan side, marking the moment of passage. Peter's image of the church as 'living stones being built up as a spiritual house' (1 Pet 2:5) draws on the memorial-stone tradition: the church is a collected witness to the passage through death into new life, just as the Jordan stones memorialized Israel's crossing.

2.note: The generation born in the wilderness had not been circumcised — upon entering the land, God commands the rite resumed. Paul identifies the antitype: 'in him also you were circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, by putting off the body of the flesh, by the circumcision of Christ.' The Gilgal circumcision marks the entry into the promised land; the 'circumcision of Christ' marks entry into the new life of the Spirit.

12.note: The manna ceases the day after Israel eats the produce of the land — the wilderness provision ends when the promise is reached. Paul's Passover proclamation ('Christ our Passover has been sacrificed') connects to this transition: the shadow-feasts of the wilderness (Passover, manna) give way to the reality. When the type is fulfilled, the type is no longer needed; the church celebrates not the wilderness feast but the feast of fulfillment.

13.note: The commander of the LORD's army appears to Joshua with drawn sword — neither 'for us nor for our adversaries' but as the LORD's own general. The scene is a Christophany: the warrior who takes command of the conquest from Joshua himself. Revelation 19:11-14 reveals the antitype: the rider on the white horse, clothed in a robe dipped in blood, leads the armies of heaven into the final battle. The commander outside Jericho is the same one who leads the ultimate campaign.

The commander who takes over Joshua's mission is the divine warrior — the pioneer who goes ahead. Hebrews 2:10 calls Jesus 'the founder [archēgon — pioneer, captain] of their salvation.' The vocabulary of military leadership (Joshua is to remove his sandals and defer to the commander) is the OT embodiment of what Hebrews says about Christ: he is the captain, we follow in the path he opened.

The commander of YHWH's army with his drawn sword — the divine warrior who appears to Joshua is the pre-incarnate Christ, the same figure who leads the armies of heaven in Revelation 19; the Joshua narrative is set in motion by the personal presence of the divine commander

15.note: 'Take off your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy.' The same command given to Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3:5, quoted by Stephen in Acts 7:33) is now given to Joshua before Jericho. The repetition of the holy-ground command marks both commissions as divine encounters — the same God who sent Moses now sends Joshua, and the same holiness-demand attends both theophanies.

20.note: By faith the walls of Jericho fell, after the army had marched around them for seven days — Hebrews reads the Jericho conquest as a faith-type: the victory came not through siege weapons but through trusting YHWH's word and executing his seemingly irrational command. The author places it in the same faith-catalogue as Abraham's obedience, locating military victory inside the same theological category as the patriarchal promises.

22.note: By faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she had given a friendly welcome to the spies — Hebrews explicitly names Rahab as a faith-type alongside the patriarchs. Her scarlet cord (v18) and her reception of the spies are the acts of faith; her survival when the city fell is the reward. She models Gentile inclusion in the covenant people on the basis of faith, not birth.

Was not Rahab the prostitute also justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way? — James uses Rahab to demonstrate that faith without works is dead: her faith (trust in YHWH and his agents) produced works (protecting the spies, marking her house, gathering her family). James and Hebrews agree on the same text while stressing complementary aspects of the same complete faith.

1.note: Do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump? — YHWH's anger falls on all Israel because Achan alone violated the herem (v1: the Israelites broke faith with YHWH); the community is treated as a single moral organism in which one member's sin corrupts the whole. Paul invokes this same corporate-holiness principle when warning Corinth that tolerating known sin in the congregation puts the entire community at risk.

26.note: I will make the Valley of Achor a door of hope — the Valley of Achor is named for the trouble Achan brought on Israel (achor = trouble); it becomes a place of stoning and curse. Hosea redeems the name: in the new-covenant restoration, YHWH will transform Israel's most prominent site of covenant-failure and judgment into the entry-point of hope. The most shameful place in the conquest narrative becomes the emblem of eschatological renewal.

33.note: Ezra brought the law before the assembly, men and women and all who could understand what they heard — the covenant renewal at Mt. Ebal (Josh 8:33-35) includes all Israel without distinction: men, women, children, and strangers living among them. Ezra's post-exilic covenant renewal in Neh 8 replicates the same universal-inclusion: the entire assembled people hear the Law read. Each reading-event marks a fresh national beginning under the Mosaic covenant.

23.note: But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ — the Gibeonites were foreigners who lied their way into a treaty with Israel, yet they ended up serving permanently at YHWH's altar (v27: woodcutters and water-carriers for the altar). Their outsider status was transformed into insider service. Paul's description of Gentiles who were once far off and are now brought near is the NT fulfillment-pattern of which the Gibeonites are the earliest type.

13.note: The sun and moon stood still in their place at the light of your arrows — Habakkuk's warrior-theophany hymn references (or draws on) the same tradition as the long day of Joshua 10:12-13. Habakkuk casts YHWH as the divine warrior whose cosmic interventions suspend the normal order; the sun-stopping at Gibeon becomes one of the premier examples of YHWH's direct intervention in the holy war.

25.note: Be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might — Joshua's command to the officers not to be afraid but to be strong and courageous (v25, echoing YHWH's word to Joshua in Josh 1:6-7) is the formative language of Deuteronomic military theology. Paul's armor-of-God passage in Eph 6 draws on the same vocabulary (be strong, stand firm, divine equipping) to describe the spiritual warfare of the church; the conquest courage-formula becomes the metaphor for Christian endurance.

20.note: So then he has mercy on whomever he wills, and he hardens whomever he wills — Josh 11:20 states that it was YHWH who hardened the hearts of the Canaanite nations so that they would come against Israel, receive no mercy, and be devoted to destruction. Paul's theology of divine hardening in Romans 9 is grounded in the Exodus-Pharaoh narrative, but the Joshua 11 pattern shows the same principle applied to the conquest. The hardening serves YHWH's purposes without reducing human culpability for rejection.

7.note: The catalog of thirty-one defeated Canaanite kings is the paradigm of conquest: one by one, the enemies of God's people are overcome and their territories claimed. Paul declares the ultimate antitype: 'he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet' (1 Cor 15:25). Joshua's conquest list is the OT picture of what Christ's resurrection has begun and his return will complete — total subjugation of every hostile power.

The kings who resisted Israel's advance and were defeated before the ark of the covenant anticipate 'the kings of the earth with their armies, gathered to make war against him who was sitting on the horse and against his army' (Rev 19:19) — who are likewise defeated. The pattern of earthly kings arrayed against the LORD's anointed runs from Canaan to the Apocalypse.

1.note: 'There remains yet very much land to possess' — even at Joshua's old age, after the major campaigns, the inheritance was only partially realized. The author of Hebrews cites this incompleteness as his argument: 'if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on. So then, there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God' (Heb 4:8-9). The land-rest Joshua administered was real but partial; it pointed to an eschatological rest that Joshua could not deliver.

7.note: Caleb recounts his forty-five years of faithful waiting: 'I was forty years old when Moses the servant of the LORD sent me...and I wholly followed the LORD my God' (v.8). After all those years of wilderness wandering and waiting for others to die, Caleb arrives to claim his inheritance at eighty-five years old. Hebrews 6:12 calls the community to 'imitate those who through faith and patience inherit the promises' — Caleb is the OT embodiment of this patient, persevering faith.

Caleb 'wholly followed the LORD' for forty-five years — his confidence in God's promise (Num 14:24: 'my servant Caleb has a different spirit') sustained him through the entire wilderness generation's collapse. This is Hebrews 11:1 enacted: 'faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.' Caleb never saw the inheritance during the wilderness years, yet he held it as certain and claimed it as his own the moment the door opened.

11.note: Caleb at eighty-five: 'I am still as strong today as I was in the day that Moses sent me; my strength now is as my strength was then.' The outer man has not determined the inner man — the faith-vigor of the forty-year-old spy remained undimmed. Paul's principle 'though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day' (2 Cor 4:16) is Caleb's biography: physical age did not diminish his capacity for the kingdom's demands.

45.note: Not one word of all the good promises that YHWH had made to the house of Israel had failed — Joshua's testimony of YHWH's complete faithfulness to his land-promises; yet Hebrews argues that if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on (Ps 95:7-8): the Canaan rest is real but not final, pointing to the eschatological sabbath-rest that remains for the people of God
